title: FMGE/NEXT Exam After MBBS Abroad Explained (2026) – Complete Guide for Indian Students
content:
FMGE/NEXT Exam After MBBS Abroad Explained (2026) – Complete Guide for Indian Students

Every year, thousands of Indian students choose MBBS abroad because of high competition and limited government seats in India. But the most important question which decides your future. “Will you be able to become a doctor in India after studying abroad?”

If you pursue MBBS abroad, then holding a degree is not enough. You have to clear the licensing exam for practice in India. Now known as NEXT (National Exit Test), but the system is changing. 

This simply means that if you don’t clear the NEXT exam, you won’t be able to practice law in India no matter how good a university you graduated from. Therefore, the plan shouldn’t just be to go abroad, but also to have a strategy for clearing NEXT.

Smart students are those who start NEXT-oriented preparation from day one. The right country, the right university, and studying according to the Indian syllabus all these factors are very important.

What is FMGE?

The Foreign Medical Graduate Examination (FMGE) is a licensing exam for students who have completed their MBBS outside India. Passing this exam is mandatory if you wish to practice as a doctor in India. The FMGE is conducted by the National Board of Examinations (NBE) and is held twice a year. Its purpose is to ensure that foreign graduates meet Indian medical standards. Only after clearing the FMGE exam are you eligible for registration with the Medical Council.

Key Points:

  • Mandatory to practice medicine in India
  • Conducted twice a year (June & December)
  • Screening test (pass/fail only)
  • Passing marks: 150 out of 300

What is the NEXT Exam (2026 Update)?

National Exit Test (NExT) is a proposed licensing and postgraduate entrance exam for MBBS students, which will replace NEET-PG and FMGE in future. It was originally planned to be implemented sooner, but as of April 2026, it is in a transition phase, mock tests and a phased rollout are being discussed, and NEET-PG 2026 will continue. NEXT will be in two steps: Step 1 (theory, mid-2026) and Step 2 (practical).

Key 2026 Updates and Structure

Purpose:
This will be a single, uniform exam for both medical licensing and PG seat ranking.

  • Step 1 (Theory):There will be a computer-based exam covering pre-clinical, para-clinical, and clinical subjects. The focus will be on problem-solving, case-based questions, and practical knowledge.
  • Step 2 (Practical):Will be conducted after an internship, where clinical skills and patient management will be assessed.
  • 2026 Status:There is a debate on immediate versus phased implementation until April 2026. Reports suggest the current focus is on infrastructure and system readiness, and the 2022 batch is expected to be the first batch to offer NExT.
  • Structure:NexT focuses more on conceptual clarity and clinical application rather than rote learning. It will cover major subjects such as Pathology, Pharmacology, Surgery, and Pediatrics.

FMGE vs NEXT – What’s Changing?

    • Scope & Applicability: FMGE is only for Foreign Medical Graduates (FMGs). NExT applies to all medical students (Indian & Foreign), creating a uniform standard.
    • Purpose: FMGE is a screening test to practice. NExT serves three purposes: licensure to practice, ranking for PG entrance (replacing NEET-PG), and a bridge to the internship.
  • Structure:
    • FMGE: Single-stage, computer-based, theoretical test.
    • NExT: Two-step process. Step 1 is a theoretical (computer-based) exam, and Step 2 is a practical/clinical skill assessment.
  • Difficulty/Focus: NExT will focus heavily on clinical application and competency, whereas FMGE is more theory-focused.
  • Attempts: NExT Step 1 allows for multiple attempts to improve scores, while FMGE has limited attempts. 

Eligibility for FMGE/NEXT After MBBS Abroad

To be eligible for FMGE/NEXT Exam 2026 after MBBS abroad, candidates must be Indian citizens/OCI cardholders who have completed at least a 54-month MBBS course in English from an NMC-recognized university, completed a 12-month internship in the same country, and qualified NEET-UG before starting their studies.

Essential Eligibility Criteria (NMC Guidelines 2026-27)

  • Academic Duration: The course must be at least 54 months (4.5 years), followed by a 12-month internship, both of which must be completed at the same foreign institution.
  • Medium of Instruction: The entire curriculum, including lectures and clinical training, must be in English.
  • Registration: Graduates must have a license to practice medicine in the country of study.
  • Eligibility Certificate: Candidates must obtain an eligibility certificate from the National Medical Commission (NMC) before joining a foreign university.
  • NEET Requirement: Qualifying NEET-UG is required before enrolling in an MBBS program.
  • Degree Recognition: The university must be recognized by the World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS).

Exam Details

  • Format: The exam is a computer-based test (CBT) consisting of 300 multiple-choice questions (MCQs) covering 19 subjects. Passing Score: A minimum of 50% (150/300) marks is required to qualify.
  • When: The National Board of Examinations (NBE) conducts the exam twice a year, usually in June and December.

Challenges Students Face After MBBS Abroad

  • Low FMGE pass percentage (~10–25%)
  • Lack of clinical exposure
  • Language barriers
  • Poor guidance during preparation
  • Confusion about NEXT pattern

Conclusion

Choosing MBBS abroad can open doors to your dream of becoming a doctor, but your journey doesn’t end with your degree it begins with clearing FMGE or NEXT.

With the transition towards NEXT in 2026, the process is becoming more structured, competitive, and skill-focused. This means students must be more strategic than ever from selecting the right university to preparing consistently throughout their course.

FAQs 

1. Is FMGE compulsory after MBBS abroad?

Yes, as of now FMGE is mandatory to practice in India after completing MBBS from abroad. However, it is expected to be replaced by NEXT from 2026.

2. Will NEXT completely replace FMGE in 2026?

Most likely yes. NEXT is designed to become a single, unified exam for licensing, final-year assessment, and PG admissions.

3. Is NEXT tougher than FMGE?

Yes, NEXT is expected to be more challenging because:

  • It tests concepts deeply
  • Includes practical/clinical skills (NEXT 2)
  • Also determines PG ranking

4. What is the passing mark for FMGE?

You need to score 150 out of 300 (50%) to pass FMGE.

5. How many attempts are allowed in FMGE/NEXT?

  • FMGE: Unlimited attempts
  • NEXT: Likely to have limited attempts (final rules may vary)

6. Can I do PG in India after MBBS abroad?

Yes, but only after:

  • Clearing FMGE/NEXT
  • Completing internship requirements

After that, you can apply for PG through NEXT (or NEET-PG if applicable).

7. Which countries are best for MBBS abroad for Indian students?

Popular options include:

  • Russia
  • Georgia
  • Kazakhstan
  • Philippines

But the “best” country depends on your budget, university quality, and FMGE/NEXT success rate.

8. What is the FMGE pass percentage?

It is generally low, around 10–25%, which shows the importance of proper preparation and guidance.

9. When should I start preparing for FMGE/NEXT?

Ideally from 2nd or 3rd year of your MBBS to build strong concepts and avoid last-minute pressure.

10. What is the biggest mistake students make in MBBS abroad?

Choosing the wrong university without checking:

  • NMC recognition
  • FMGE results
  • Clinical exposure

This can seriously impact your chances of practicing in India.

title: MBBS Seats in India Total 2026
content:
MBBS Seats in India Total 2026

Every year lakhs of students appear for NEET exam, but only a few percentage of students secure a MBBS seat. The competition is intense because demand is high and seats are limited. If you’re planning for 2026, then understanding “total MBBS seats in India 2026” is very important, so you can smartly plan your strategy. 

By 2026, the number of MBBS seats in India is expected to increase further, thanks to new medical colleges and government initiatives. Based on current trends, there is a possibility of more than 1 lakh MBBS seats being available in India, including both government and private colleges. Government colleges have lower fees and higher competition, while private colleges have higher fees but cutoffs may be comparatively lower.

You need to understand that knowing only total seats is not enough. You have to understand the distribution of All India Quota (AIQ) and State Quota. 15% of seats are filled through AIQ, and the remaining 85% fall under state quota. This means that your domicile state also impacts your chances.

This guide will help you understand the MBBS Seats in India Total 2026,their scope, salary, and career opportunities so you can make a practical decision.

Total MBBS Seats in India 2026

If you are preparing for NEET 2026, it is very important to understand the updated data of MBBS seats. As of April 2026, the total MBBS seats in India have reached approximately 1,29,603, which are available in 824+ medical colleges. This number has grown at a very fast pace in the last few years, mainly due to government initiatives and infrastructure expansion.

MBBS Seats in India 2026: Latest Data & Trends (List Format)

  • Total MBBS Seats (2026): ~129,603
  • Total Medical Colleges: 824+

Seat Distribution:

  • Government College Seats: ~63,683
  • Private/Deemed College Seats: ~65,920
  • Government vs Private Ratio: Approximately 49:51

Key Trends (2023–2026):

  • Rapid Expansion:
    • 43 new medical colleges approved for the 2025–26 session
    • 11,682+ new MBBS seats added
  • Growth Comparison:
    • Around 108,000+ seats in 2023
    • Increased to ~129,603 (nearly 1.3 lakh) by 2026
  • Infrastructure Development:
    • Strong growth in medical education infrastructure
    • Expansion focused on Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities
  • District-Level Expansion:
    • Goal: At least one government medical college in every district
    • Aim to improve rural healthcare access and education opportunities

Key Insight:

  • The number of seats is increasing, but NEET competition remains high
  • Government seats are limited and highly competitive
  • Private colleges offer an alternative pathway for many students

MBBS Seats in India 2026: Government vs Private Colleges

As of 2026, there are over 1.1 lakh MBBS seats in India, roughly divided between government and private colleges. Government colleges offer very competitive, affordable tuition (approximately ₹10k-₹50k per year) with 55k to 60k+ seats. Private colleges offer more seats (~50k+ seats), often with better modern infrastructure, but their fees are significantly higher, ranging from ₹10 lakh to over ₹25 lakh per year.

Total MBBS Seats (2026): 1.1 lakh+

Overall Split: Almost equal distribution between government and private colleges

Government Colleges (Public Medical Colleges)

Number of Seats: Approx 55,000–60,000+ (approx 50%)

Top Institutes Included: AIIMS & central institutes

Fees Structure:

  • very affordable
  • Approximately ₹10,000 – ₹50,000 per year

Admission Criteria:

  • NEET-UG based (high merit required)
  • 15% All india Quota (AIQ)
  • 85% State Quota

Competition Level:

Extremely high due to low fees and limited seats

Private Colleges (Private & Deemed Universities)

Number of Seats: 50,000+

Fee Structure:

  • Quite expensive
  • Approximately ₹10 lakh – ₹25 lakh+ per year
  • Fees are higher in the Management/NRI quota

Admission Criteria:

NEET required, but cutoffs are relatively lower

Availability:

  • Admission chances are higher than at government colleges
  • Some top private colleges are still highly competitive

State-wise MBBS Seats in India 2026: Top States with Highest Seats

  • Tamil Nadu
    • One of the highest MBBS seat providers in India
    • Strong government focus on public healthcare and medical education
    • Large number of government medical colleges across districts
    • Well-established healthcare infrastructure and early adoption of expansion policies
  • Karnataka
    • High number of private and deemed medical universities
    • Considered a hub for medical education in India
    • Attracts students from across the country due to wide availability of seats
    • Balanced mix of government and private institutions
  • Maharashtra
    • Large population leading to higher demand for medical education
    • Significant number of both government and private colleges
    • Continuous expansion in medical infrastructure
    • Strong presence of reputed institutions in major cities
  • Uttar Pradesh
    • Rapid growth in the number of government medical colleges in recent years
    • Government initiative to establish colleges in multiple districts
    • One of the largest states, resulting in high seat capacity
    • Increasing focus on improving healthcare access and education

Why These States Lead in MBBS Seats

  • Higher Population Demand: Larger states require more doctors, leading to more colleges and seats
  • Government Investment: Strong policies to expand medical education infrastructure
  • Private Sector Contribution: Especially in states like Karnataka, private colleges boost total seats
  • Better Infrastructure: Established healthcare systems support medical training and hospitals

Conclusion

The total MBBS Seats in India Total 2026 reflect steady growth due to the government’s focus on expanding medical education. However, despite the increase in seats, the competition remains extremely high because of the rising number of NEET aspirants every year. Government colleges offer limited seats with high cut-offs, while private colleges provide more availability but at a higher cost.

FAQs

  1. How many MBBS seats are there in India in 2026?
     The total number of MBBS Seats in India Total 2026 is expected to cross 1.15 lakh seats, including both government and private medical colleges, with numbers increasing every year.
  2. How many government MBBS seats are available in India?
     Approximately 55,000–60,000 seats are available in government medical colleges, which are highly competitive due to lower fees and better infrastructure.
  3. Is it difficult to get an MBBS seat in India?
     Yes, securing a seat is challenging because lakhs of students appear for NEET, while the MBBS Seats in India Total 2026 are limited in comparison.
  4. Which state has the highest MBBS seats in India?
     States like Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Maharashtra, and Uttar Pradesh have the highest number of MBBS seats due to a larger number of medical colleges.
  5. What are the chances of getting MBBS with a low NEET score?
     With a low NEET score, getting a government seat is difficult. However, students can explore private colleges, deemed universities, or MBBS abroad options.
  6. Do MBBS seats increase every year in India?
     Yes, the government is continuously approving new medical colleges and expanding existing ones, which increases the total number of seats annually.

7. What should I do if I don’t get an MBBS seat in India?
 You can consider options like private medical colleges, allied health courses, or applying for MBBS abroad in countries with affordable fees and globally recognized degrees.

title: Best Time to Apply for MBBS Abroad 2026 Intake (Complete Guide for Indian Students)
content:
Best Time to Apply for MBBS Abroad 2026 Intake (Complete Guide for Indian Students)

If you’re planning to pursue an MBBS abroad in 2026, understand that timing isn’t everything. Many students miss out on their dream colleges because they apply late or lack proper guidance.

The truth is that MBBS abroad admissions are on a first come, first served basis. This means that students who apply early have a better chance of securing a seat in top universities. Late applicants often get limited options or have to compromise on college quality.

Another major problem is incomplete information. Many students don’t know when the application process starts, what documents are required, and how to apply. This leads them to miss deadlines or make last-minute mistakes in a rush.

So, if you’re serious about MBBS abroad, start planning early. Applying at the right time, choosing the right university, and taking proper guidance  all these factors decide your success. 

Perfect Best Time to Apply for MBBS Abroad 2026 

If you’re considering MBBS abroad for admission in 2026, it is very important to understand this perfect timeline because even a small delay can keep you away from top universities.

April – May 2026: Application Phase

Admissions officially begin in mid-April (around April 15th), especially in countries like Russia, Kazakhstan, and Georgia. You should have your documents ready by this time 10th/12th grade mark sheets, passport, etc. Qualifying NEET is also mandatory; admission is not possible without it.

June 2026: High-Demand Deadline

This is the most crucial phase. Seats at top universities begin to fill by mid-June (around June 15th). Therefore, you should finalize your university by this time—considering NMC approval, fees, and location.

July 2026: Admission Confirmation

Universities issue admission letters this month (usually within 1–2 weeks). You must also submit your final NEET result to confirm eligibility.

July – August 2026: Visa Process

Now comes the visa stage first, you receive an invitation letter, then you apply for a visa at the embassy. This process takes time, so don’t delay.

August – September 2026: Final Step & Travel

After receiving your visa, complete your medicals, pay your fees, and book your flight tickets. Most universities’ sessions begin in September–October.

Basic Eligibility:

  • Age: 17+ years
  • PCB: 50% (General), 40% (Reserved)
  • NEET: Mandatory

Common Mistakes Students Make 

Students often make some common mistakes during the MBBS abroad process. The biggest mistake is applying at the last minute, which can result in missing out on top universities. Another mistake is choosing random or low-ranked universities. Making decisions based solely on fees can be risky in the future. Many students also ignore NMC guidelines, which are important for practicing in India. Delays in the visa process are also a major issue.

Benefits of applying early

Applying early for MBBS abroad offers several advantages. First, you significantly increase your chances of getting admission into top universities because seats are available right away. Second, fees are comparatively lower, as early applicants can access better fee options and scholarships.

The third advantage is getting a visa faster because you have enough time to complete the documents and process. And most importantly, the entire process becomes stress-free because you avoid last-minute rushes and confusion.

Who should apply for MBBS Abroad 2026?

MBBS abroad isn’t for every student, but it can be the best option for certain categories. First, NEET-qualified students because without NEET, you won’t be able to practice in India even after completing an MBBS abroad.

Second, students who can’t afford high private college fees in India. Many countries abroad offer affordable MBBS options, where quality education is available at a comparatively low cost.

Third, students who want international exposure to the experience of studying in a new culture, global education system, and advanced medical infrastructure can strengthen their career.

Conclusion

If you’re serious about enrolling in MBBS abroad in 2026, the most important factor is applying on time. Applying between September and December 2025 gives you a better chance of admission to top universities, keeps the process smooth, and makes visa approval easier. Applying late limits your options and can lead to unnecessary stress. Therefore, the smart approach is to plan early, seek proper guidance, and secure your seat on time so that your dream of MBBS abroad can be fulfilled without any hassle.

FAQs (Frequently Asked Questions)

  1. What is the best time to apply for MBBS abroad in 2026?

The best time is from September to December 2025, as this is the period when most universities are open and seats are readily available.

  1. Is qualifying NEET necessary for MBBS abroad?

Yes, qualifying NEET is mandatory for Indian students, especially if you want to study in India in the future.

  1. Can I apply after January 2026?

Yes, but by then, many university seats are filled, leaving you with limited options.

  1. Which country is best for MBBS abroad?

Russia, Georgia, and Kazakhstan are popular options because the fees are affordable and the education quality is good.

  1. What is the average budget for an MBBS abroad?

Generally, the total cost of an MBBS abroad ranges between 15 to 35 lakh INR, depending on the country and university.

  1. Can you practice in India after MBBS abroad?

Yes, but you must qualify the FMGE or NEXT exam to practice in India.

  1. How long does the admission process take?

Usually, the entire admission process takes 2 to 4 months, including documentation and visa.

  1. Is MBBS abroad possible without IELTS?

Yes, MBBS without IELTS is possible in many countries like Russia and Kazakhstan.

title: State Quota vs All India Quota MBBS Admission 2026 – Complete Guide
content:
State Quota vs All India Quota MBBS Admission 2026 – Complete Guide

If you’re planning to get MBBS admission in 2026, then you need to understand both the options “State Quota” and “All India Quota (AIQ)”. Both admissions are through NEET UG, but their process and competition are very high. 

Under the State Quota, approximately 85% of seats in government medical colleges in each state are reserved for students who are domiciled in that state. The advantage of this is that competition is relatively low, and cutoffs are slightly lower than AIQ. If you want to study in your home state and are domiciled there, this option can be quite beneficial.

On the other hand, the All India Quota (AIQ) represents 15% of government college seats for which students from all over India compete. Competition is quite high, but the advantage is that you can get admission into top colleges in any state, even if you don’t have a domicile there.

What is All India Quota (AIQ)?

The All India Quota (AIQ) is a centralized admission system that reserves 15% of seats in government medical colleges. Students from across India can apply for these seats, regardless of their state of residence. Admission is based on NEET UG scores, so merit is the most important factor. 

Key Points:

  • 15% of seats in government MBBS colleges in every state fall under AIQ.
  • Counseling takes place at the central level, conducted by the Medical Counseling Committee (MCC).
  • You can get admission to top government colleges in any state other than your own.
  • Competition is quite high as All India toppers compete here.

Example:

If you are from Uttar Pradesh, you can also get admission to government medical colleges in Delhi, Rajasthan, or Maharashtra through AIQ.

In simple words, AIQ is the best option for students who want to target top colleges outside their state and can handle high competition.

What is the state quota?

85% of seats in the State Quota are reserved for students from the respective state. These seats are only available to candidates with state domicile.

Key Points:

  • 85% of seats are reserved for local students.
  • Counseling is conducted by state authorities.
  • You can only apply to colleges within your state.
  • The cutoff is slightly lower than for AIQ.

Example:

If you are a domicile holder from Uttar Pradesh, you can apply to government medical colleges in Uttar Pradesh through the State Quota.

State Quota vs AIQ – Major Differences

Feature

All India Quota (AIQ)

State Quota

Seat Percentage

15%

85%

Eligibility

All India students

Only state domicile

Competition

Very High

Relatively Lower

College Options

Across India

Only within state

Counseling Authority

MCC

State Govt Authority

Which is better? (AIQ vs State Quota)

This entire decision depends on your rank, preference, and career goals. Both options State Quota and All India Quota (AIQ) are valid; you just have to figure out which one is the best fit for you. 

AIQ is better if:

  • Your NEET UG rank is quite good
  • You are targeting top institutes like the All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS) or reputed government colleges
  • You are open to studying outside your home state
  • You can handle high competition and want all-India exposure

State Quote is better if:

  • Your NEET rank is in the moderate or average range
  • You prefer to pursue MBBS in your own state (family, comfort, language, etc.)
  • You want to take advantage of relatively lower cutoffs
  • You can benefit from local reservation policies

Simply put, if you are a topper and aiming for the best colleges, AIQ is a strong option. But if you want a safe and practical option, then State Quota will be more beneficial.

Important Tips for MBBS Admission 2026

  • NEET UG score is the most important factor quota only provides opportunity; final selection depends on merit.
  • Apply to both counseling processes AIQ (central) and state counseling to maximize your selection chances.
  • Have documents ready in advance, such as domicile certificate, category certificate, marksheets, ID proof, etc.as delays during counseling can be risky.
  • Fill your choices smartly—fill your options only after analyzing your college preference, location, and past cutoffs, as this step determines final seat allotment.
  • Check official updates regularly, especially on the website of the Medical Counseling Committee (MCC) and your state counseling authority.

The simple strategy is this: a good NEET score + smart counseling decisions = strong chances of getting an MBBS seat confirmed.

Conclusion

Both State Quota vs All India Quota MBBS are important pathways to MBBS admission. AIQ gives you all-India exposure and access to top colleges, while State Quota offers relatively easier competition and local advantage. The smart strategy is to utilize both options and choose the best possible college based on your NEET rank.

FAQs

Q1. Can I apply to both AIQ and State Quota?

Yes, you can apply to both. It is also recommended.

Q2. Is domicile required for AIQ?

No, domicile is not required for AIQ.

Q3. What is required for State Quota?

You must have a valid domicile from that state.

Q4. Why is the AIQ cutoff high?

Because top students from across India compete in the same pool.

Q5. Do private colleges also have AIQ?

No, AIQ is primarily for government colleges (some deemed universities have a different process).State Quota vs All India Quota MBBS

title: NEET Dress Code 2026: Complete Guidelines, Do’s & Don’ts for Exam Day
content:
NEET Dress Code 2026: Complete Guidelines, Do’s & Don’ts for Exam Day

The NEET exam is held every year following strict guidelines, and the dress code is one of the most important. Ignoring it can lead to unnecessary stress, delays, and even risk being denied entry on exam day.

What is the NEET Dress Code 2026?

According to the NEET dress code 2026, all candidates must wear simple and basic clothing. To ensure fair, transparent, and secure examination, the NTA has established clear rules. These rules apply equally to both male and female candidates, so they must be followed.

NEET Dress Code for Males 2026

Male candidates appearing for the NEET Dress Code 2026 should wear simple and light clothing to ensure a smooth security check. Avoid clothing with extra layers, heavy designs, or metal parts, as these can make checking difficult.

Allowed Dress for Male Candidates:

  • Half-sleeve shirt or T-shirt
  • Plain and light-colored clothing
  • Simple trousers or pants (without too many pockets)
  • Slippers or open-toed sandals (with a thin sole)

Not Allowed (Strictly Avoid):

  • Full-sleeve shirts or jackets
  • Kurta or layered clothing
  • Thick-soled shoes
  • Belts, caps, watches, or any metallic accessories

Candidates should also ensure that their clothing does not have zippers, large buttons, or hidden pockets, as these can cause extra friction and lead to unnecessary delays. By following simple dressing, you can have a tension-free entry on exam day.

NEET Dress Code for Female 2026

Female candidates also need to follow NEET dress code 2026 rules strictly. The main focus is on simple comfortable and modest dressing, so that frisking can be done easily and exam integrity can be maintained.

Allowed Dress for Female Candidates:

  • Half-sleeve kurtis, tops or T-shirts
  • Simple salwar, leggings or trousers
  • Slipper or open-toed sandals (with thin sole)

Strictly Not Allowed:

  • Full-sleeve tops or dresses
  • Heavy embroidery, frills or layered clothing
  • High heels, fancy footwear or boots
  • Any type of jewellery (earrings, bangles, rings, necklaces)
  • Hair accessories like clips, pins or bands

Female candidates should avoid tight-fitting clothes and opt for simple outfits.This makes security checks faster and you don’t have to face unnecessary delays or inconveniences. Simple and comfortable dressing allows you to remain stress-free on exam day.

NEET Customary Dress Code 2026

Candidates wearing traditional or religious clothing, such as burqas or turbans, may do so. They must indicate “customary dress” on their NEET application form 2026. These candidates must arrive at the center two hour early to allow for thorough security checks.

NEET Dress Code 2026: List of Prohibited Items in the Exam Hall

The National Testing Agency (NTA) has strictly prohibited certain items for the NEET exam to prevent cheating and ensure a fair exam. It’s important to remember that storage facilities are not available at exam centers, so avoid carrying unnecessary items.

  • Electronic Devices (Strictly Prohibited):

Mobile phones, smartwatches, cameras, Bluetooth devices, earphones, pagers, microchips, calculators – none of these items are allowed.

  • Study Materials:

Any written or printed paper, notes, books, pencil boxes, or geometry boxes are strictly prohibited.

  • Accessories:

Bags, belts, caps or hats, sunglasses, wallets, and any type of jewelry (earrings, rings, chains) are not allowed.

  • Other Restricted Items:

Electronic pens, scanners, scales, erasers, and food items are also not allowed (exceptions may be made in case of a medical condition with proper documentation).

To be safe, carry only your admit card, valid ID proof, and required documents. It’s best to go with simple and minimal items to avoid any problems on exam day.

COVID-19 Guidelines for NEET 2026 (Expected)

Expected guidelines may include masks, transparent water bottles, and 50 ml hand sanitizer. Gloves may be permitted. Candidates will also need their NEET 2026 admit card, photo ID, and a signed self-declaration.

Important Exam Day Tips

  • Reach the exam centre at least 2 hours before the exam to avoid last-minute rush and stress.
  • Carry your admit card and valid ID proof, without these you will not be allowed to enter.
  • Wear simple and dress code-compliant clothes to avoid delay in security check.
  • Follow all the instructions of the exam authorities carefully, as violation of rules can create problems.

By following these simple tips, you can make your exam day smooth and stress-free.

Conclusion

The National Eligibility cum Entrance Test (NEET) dress code is designed to ensure a fair and smooth examination process for all candidates. By following the guidelines set by the National Testing Agency, students can avoid last-minute stress and focus entirely on their performance.

Always choose simple, comfortable clothing and avoid carrying restricted items to the exam center. Remember, even a small mistake in dress code can lead to unnecessary delays. Stay prepared, stay confident, and give your best in NEET 2026.

FAQs 

1. What is the dress code for NEET 2026?

Candidates must wear light-colored, simple clothes with half sleeves and avoid heavy designs, full sleeves, and accessories.

2. Can I wear jeans in NEET 2026?

Yes, simple jeans or trousers are allowed, but avoid fancy designs, embroidery, or too many pockets.

3. Are shoes allowed in the NEET exam?

No, candidates are advised to wear slippers or sandals. Shoes are generally not permitted.

4. Is jewelry allowed in NEET 2026?

No, items like earrings, rings, chains, or bracelets are strictly prohibited.

5. Can girls wear a dupatta in NEET?

In most cases, dupattas are restricted. It’s better to avoid carrying one to prevent any issues during checking.

6. What about religious dress in NEET?

Candidates wearing religious attire must inform during the application process and reach the center early for security checks.

7. What items are not allowed inside the exam hall?

Electronic devices, wallets, handbags, metallic items, and food (unless medically required) are not allowed.

8. What should I carry to the NEET exam center?

Carry your admit card, valid ID proof, and passport-size photograph as per guidelines.

title: MBBS Abroad Admission Process for Indian Students 2026: Complete Guide (Step-by-Step)
content:
MBBS Abroad Admission Process for Indian Students 2026: Complete Guide (Step-by-Step)

Pursuing MBBS abroad has become a highly popular option for Indian students these days. The main reason for this is the limited government medical seats and high competition in India. Every year, millions of students take NEET-UG, but seats are very limited. This is why students explore foreign options where admission is relatively easier and affordable.

Countries like Russia, Kazakhstan, Georgia, and the Philippines are very popular among Indian students. Their universities are globally recognized, and the fees are also much lower than those at Indian private colleges. Furthermore, due to the English-medium programs, students do not face any language barriers.

The next step is the application process, where you submit your documents—such as 10th-12th marksheets, passport, NEET scorecard, etc. The visa process begins after receiving the admission letter from the university. After visa approval, you finalize your travel planning and accommodations.

The fee structure is also an important factor. Abroad MBBS generally costs between ₹15–40 lakh (approximately $1.5 million), which is considered quite affordable compared to Indian private colleges.

In this complete guide, we will explain the MBBS abroad admission process 2026 step-by-step. First, eligibility is checked such as NEET qualification, required marks in PCB subjects, and minimum age criteria. You then select a suitable country and university based on budget, ranking, and recognition.

Why Study MBBS Abroad in 2026?

  • No donation or capitation fees: Meaning there’s no need to pay extra money or under-the-table payments to get admission. Admission is based solely on merit and eligibility.
  • Affordable tuition fees: The cost of studying here is quite reasonable compared to private colleges in India. It’s manageable even for middle-class students.
  • Globally recognized universities (WHO, NMC approved): Degrees from universities recognized by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the National Medical Commission (NMC) are valid worldwide. You can practice both in India and abroad (following the rules).
  • English-medium programs: The entire course is in English, so there’s no language barrier. It’s easier for international students to understand and adapt.
  • High FMGE/Next success potential: If the university’s curriculum is strong, the chances of clearing the FMGE/Next increase. This helps in obtaining a license in India.

Eligibility Criteria for MBBS Abroad (2026)

For 2026, Indian students must be at least 17 years old by December 31st of the admission year to be eligible for MBBS abroad, must have passed 12th grade with 50% marks in PCB (40% for reserved categories), and must have passed the NEET-UG exam (MANDATORY). The degree must be 54+ months long, include a 12-month internship at the same institution, and be taught 100% in English.

Key Eligibility & Regulations (2026)

  • NEET Mandatory

If you want to become a doctor in India in the future, clearing NEET-UG is compulsory. This rule is according to the National Medical Commission (NMC). Without NEET, you will not be able to practice in India.

  • Academic Qualifications

A minimum of 50% marks in Physics, Chemistry, and Biology (PCB) in 12th grade (40% for SC/ST/OBC) are required. This is the basic eligibility for MBBS abroad.

  • Age Limit

Students must be at least 17 years old by December 31, 2026. Admission before that time is not valid.

NMC/FMGL Regulations 2026

  • Course Duration

The MBBS course must be a minimum of 54 months (approximately 4.5 years). Courses with a shorter duration than this will not be considered valid in India.

  • Internship

It is mandatory to complete a 12-month internship at the same foreign university/hospital. A separate internship in India will not be accepted if it is not completed there.

  • Language

The entire course must be in English medium. Mixed or local language programs are not accepted.

  • License Requirement

The degree you are pursuing must grant you a license to practice as a doctor in that country. If you do not receive the right to practice there, this may be an issue in India (especially during FMGE/future licensing exams).

MBBS Abroad Fees Structure (Approx 2026)

Country

Fees (Per Year)

Russia

₹2.5 – ₹5 Lakhs

Kazakhstan

₹2 – ₹4 Lakhs

Georgia

₹4 – ₹7 Lakhs

Philippines

₹3 – ₹6 Lakhs

Top Countries for MBBS Abroad

Russia

  • Affordable and experienced universities
  • Strong Indian student presence

Kazakhstan

  • Budget-friendly option
  • Fast admission process

Georgia

  • European standard education
  • Better infrastructure

Philippines

  • US-based curriculum
  • High FMGE success rate

Tips to Secure MBBS Admission in 2026

  • Apply early (May–August intake)
  • Verify NMC-approved universities
  • Avoid agents without credentials
  • Check FMGE results of universities
  • Prepare finances in advance

Conclusion

MBBS abroad 2026 is a smart and practical option for Indian students, especially given the limited seats and high competition in India. If you choose the right country, an NMC-approved university, and receive proper guidance, you can receive quality medical education at affordable fees.

The most important thing is to qualify NEET and follow the National Medical Commission’s guidelines. Early planning, correct documentation, and trusted consultations can make your admission process smooth.

FAQs

  1. Is MBBS abroad possible without NEET?

No, qualifying NEET is compulsory for Indian students, otherwise you will not be able to practice in India.

  1. What is the total cost of MBBS abroad?

Generally ₹15 lakh to ₹40 lakh, depending on the country and university.

  1. Is an MBBS abroad degree valid in India?

Yes, if you study at a National Medical Commission-approved university and clear the FMGE/NexT exam.

  1. Which country is best for MBBS?

Russia, Kazakhstan, Georgia, and the Philippines are popular options—depending on budget and preference.

  1. When does the admission process start?

Usually, the process starts from May–June and intake is from September–October.

  1. Is MBBS abroad safe for Indian students?

Yes, if you choose recognized universities and safe countries.

  1. Is part-time work allowed?

Allowed in some countries, but the MBBS schedule is quite hectic.

title: Top Government Medical Colleges in India 2026 (NEET Rank-wise List, Fees, Cutoff & Admission Guide)
content:
Top Government Medical Colleges in India 2026 (NEET Rank-wise List, Fees, Cutoff & Admission Guide)

Every year, lakhs of students appear for NEET UG with the dream of gaining admission to one of India’s top government medical colleges. Competition is expected to be tough, as seats are limited and applicants are numerous.

If we talk about the best colleges, AIIMS New Delhi tops the list, known for its top NIRF ranking, world-class faculty, and advanced research facilities. This is followed by PGIMER Chandigarh, renowned for its clinical exposure and training. JIPMER Puducherry is also a top choice in South India, offering quality education and modern infrastructure.

The biggest advantage of these government colleges is their affordable fees some institutions charge as little as ₹5,000 per year. The admission process is primarily through NEET UG, followed by seat allotment through All India Quote and State Counseling.

Choosing the right college in 2026 is crucial. Students should consider their NEET rank, cutoff trends, college reputation, location, and facilities before making their decision. With proper planning and research, you can fulfill your MBBS dream from a top government college.

NEET UG 2026 Counselling Process (All India Quota – AIQ)

The admission process of NEET UG conducted by the Medical Counselling Committee (MCC) through official website mcc.nic.in,

Important Notes

  • AIQ covers 15% seats in government medical colleges across India
  • Remaining 85% seats are filled through State Counselling
  • Missing deadlines = automatic disqualification for that round

Conclusion

Getting into a top government medical college in India in 2026 is highly competitive but absolutely achievable with the right preparation and strategy. Your performance in NEET UG plays the most crucial role, but smart counselling decisions, understanding cutoffs, and choosing the right colleges can make a huge difference in your final selection.Government medical colleges not only offer world-class education at very low fees, but also provide excellent clinical exposure and career opportunities.

 Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

1. What is the best government medical college in India in 2026?

The top position is consistently held by All India Institute of Medical Sciences Delhi due to its excellent faculty, infrastructure, and research facilities.

2. What NEET rank is required for top government colleges?

For top institutes like AIIMS Delhi, you typically need a rank under 50. For other leading colleges, a rank within 500–2000 is generally required.

3. How many government MBBS seats are available in India?

India has over 55,000+ government MBBS seats, but the exact number may increase slightly in 2026 due to new colleges and seat expansion.

4. What is the fee for government medical colleges?

Fees are very affordable, ranging from ₹5,000 to ₹1 lakh per year depending on the college.

5. Is admission only through NEET UG?

Yes, admission to all government MBBS colleges in India is done through NEET UG only.

6. What is the role of counselling in MBBS admission?

After qualifying NEET, you must participate in counselling conducted by the Medical Counselling Committee (for All India Quota) and respective state authorities.

7. Can I get a government medical college with 500 marks?

It depends on your category, state quota, and cutoff trends. In some states, 500 marks may be enough for lower-ranked government colleges.

8. Which is better: AIQ or State Quota?

Both have advantages.

  • AIQ (15%) gives access to top colleges across India
  • State quota (85%) may have lower cutoffs depending on your domicile

9. Are government medical colleges better than private?

Yes, in most cases. Government colleges offer:

  • Lower fees
  • Better patient exposure
  • Strong reputation

10. How can I predict which college I will get?

You can use a college predictor tool or get expert counselling based on your:

  • NEET rank
  • Category
  • State domicile

title: MBBS Fees in Private Colleges in India 2026 – Low Fees Options Available | Apply Now
content:
MBBS Fees in Private Colleges in India 2026 – Low Fees Options Available | Apply Now

Pursuing MBBS in India is considered a biggest achievement, especially when it comes to private colleges. Generally, MBBS fees in private colleges in India range from  ₹60 lakh to ₹1.5 crore even more. If we talk about yearly tuition fees, then it usually costs ₹10 lakh to ₹25 lakh per year and also depends on college and state. 

Deemed universities have a very high structure. Annual fees at these institutions often exceed ₹20 lakh or more, and the total cost can easily exceed ₹1 crore. Universities charge fees based on their facilities, infrastructure, and brand value.

On the other hand, there are some private medical colleges that offer relatively affordable options. In Karnataka, West Bengal, and some other states, fees start from ₹6 lakh to ₹9 lakh per annum, especially for state quota or merit-based seats. However, fees for management quota or NRI quota seats are significantly higher.

Students should consider not only tuition fees, but also hostel charges, miscellaneous fees, and examination fees when considering MBBS admission. Overall, pursuing MBBS in private colleges can be costly, but with proper research and planning, students can choose the best option according to their budget.

MBBS Fees in Private Colleges (2026 Overview)

If you’re planning to pursue MBBS from a private college in 2026, it’s crucial to understand the fee structure. Generally, annual tuition fees at private medical colleges range from ₹10 lakh to ₹20 lakh+. These fees can be even higher at top-tier private colleges or deemed universities.

Taking into account the total course cost, the full MBBS (5.5 years) typically costs ₹60 lakh to ₹1 crore+. This includes tuition, hostel, mess, and other charges.

Location-wise fees also vary:

  • Uttar Pradesh: ₹11 lakh to ₹12.5 lakh per year, totaling ₹60–₹90 lakh
  • Delhi/NCR: ₹15 lakh to ₹30 lakh per year for management quota seats
  • Low-cost exception: Some reputable colleges, such as CMC Vellore, have fees as low as ₹1 lakh per year.

In addition, students should also keep in mind additional charges. Hostel, mess, and “development fees” can add up to an additional ₹3 lakh to ₹5 lakh+ per year.

Management and NRI quota seats have a significantly higher fee structure, where a donation may be required along with tuition. These fees are significantly higher than state counseling seats.

Important points (for 2026):

Verify the full fee structure before seeking admission, as hidden charges significantly increase the total cost.

If you leave the seat midway, you may incur a heavy penalty, sometimes even paying the entire 5.5 years’ worth of fees.

Therefore, proper research and financial planning are crucial before seeking MBBS admission.

State-Wise Affordable Options (MBBS Fees in Private Colleges in India)

Affordable private MBBS options in India (2026) are mostly found in states like Karnataka (₹9–12 lakh/year), Kerala (approximately ₹7–9 lakh/year), and Uttar Pradesh (₹11–14 lakh/year), which offer significantly lower tuition fees than others. Odisha offers lower fees, but it is a “closed state” for residents.

Key States with Lower Private MBBS Fees (2026):

  • Kerala: Private MBBS fees in Kerala are quite reasonable, roughly ₹7–9 lakh per year. It is considered one of the most affordable private options in India, with strong education quality.
  • Karnataka: Karnataka is known for its good clinical exposure and stable fee structure. Fees here generally range around ₹10.5–11.5 lakh per year, which is a balanced option for both cost and quality.
  • Uttar Pradesh (UP):UP is a popular “open state” where students from other states can also apply. Fees here range between ₹11–14 lakh per year, and the admission process is considered relatively transparent.
  • Madhya Pradesh & Bihar:Private MBBS fees in these states also range from ₹10–13 lakh per year. These states offer budget-friendly options, especially for students who wish to study in North India.
  • Odisha: Fees in Odisha start quite low, around ₹6.5 lakh per year. However, it is a generally closed state, meaning admission is mostly restricted to state-domiciled students.

Hidden Costs You Must Know (MBBS Fees in Private Colleges in India)

When applying for MBBS admission, just looking at tuition fees isn’t enough. There are many hidden or additional costs that significantly increase the total expense. Therefore, it’s important to understand these things beforehand.

  • Hostel Charges: If you opt for a college hostel, this incurs separate expenses. Generally, hostel and mess charges can range from ₹1.5 lakh to ₹3 lakh per year, depending on facilities and location.
  • Security Deposit: A refundable security deposit is taken at the time of admission. It can range from ₹50,000 to ₹2 lakh. This amount is refunded after completion of the course (as per terms and conditions).
  • Miscellaneous Charges: This category includes lab fees, library charges, uniforms, ID cards, and development fees. Together, these can add up to an additional ₹50,000 to ₹1.5 lakh per year.
  • Exam Fees: Exam fees are also required each year or semester. The amount varies by college or university, but generally ranges from ₹20,000 to ₹50,000 per year.

Who Should Apply (MBBS Fees in Private Colleges in India)

Private medical colleges can be a practical option for students who want to pursue MBBS but cannot secure a government seat. This option is especially useful for those who want to choose the best possible college based on their budget and score.

  • NEET-Qualified Students

The first requirement is to qualify for the NEET. Nowadays, NEET is mandatory for MBBS admission, whether you choose a government or private college.

  • Students with Average NEET Scores

If your NEET score is slightly lower than the cutoffs of top government colleges, private colleges offer a good alternative. Here, you may find merit-based or state quota seats where competition is relatively manageable.

  • Families Looking for Budget-Friendly MBBS Options

Private MBBS is expensive, but not every college has the same fees. Some states and colleges offer relatively affordable options. This option is suitable for families who want to explore budget-friendly private MBBS with smart planning.

How We Help You 

AR Group of Education is a trusted and reputed educational consultancy that has been providing guidance to students since 2005. With our vast experience and strong network, we have established a trusted name in India’s education sector. We have successfully guided thousands of students to reach their dream medical colleges.

The MBBS admission process can be complex, but we make this journey simple and transparent. We provide personalized counseling, college selection, and complete admission support so that every student can make the best decision based on their budget and NEET score.

FAQs 

Q1. What is the total cost of MBBS in private colleges?
 The total cost of MBBS in private colleges generally ranges from ₹60 lakh to ₹1 crore+, depending on the college and state.

Q2. Is it possible to study MBBS without donation?
 Yes, it is possible through NEET-based merit admissions, especially under state quota seats where no donation is required.

Q3. Which states offer more affordable private MBBS options?
 States like Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and Uttar Pradesh are known for relatively affordable private MBBS fees.

Q4. Are hostel and other charges included in tuition fees?
 No, hostel, mess, and miscellaneous charges are separate and can add around ₹2–₹5 lakh per year.

Q5. What is a management quota?
 Management quota seats are offered by private colleges with higher tuition fees, and sometimes additional donation may be required.

Q6. Is NEET qualification mandatory for private MBBS?
 Yes, qualifying NEET is mandatory for MBBS admission in both government and private colleges in India.

Q7. Is there any penalty for leaving the course midway?
 Yes, many colleges impose a heavy penalty if you leave the course mid-way, sometimes equal to the full course fee.

Q8. Can I get an education loan for private MBBS?
 Yes, many banks offer education loans for MBBS, subject to eligibility criteria.

title: MBBS Admission 2026 Without Donation – Apply Now | Limited Seats
content:
MBBS Admission 2026 Without Donation – Apply Now | Limited Seats

The demand for MBBS is increasing every year. The reason is simple, the population is increasing and the healthcare sector is rapidly growing. Every student wants to become a doctor because this profession provides respect, stability and good income. 

But the problem is that MBBS seats are limited and competition is very high. Every year lakhs of students appear for NEET, but seats are comparatively less. Due to this reason students and parents often get confused and pressured. 

Worried about ₹50 lakh donation?

Many people believe that donation is compulsory for admission to MBBS but this is not completely true.

Reality is:

  • There is zero donation in Government colleges only merit matters
  • Even in Private colleges donation is not needed many seats are available through counselling & merit quota.
  • Due to lack of proper guidance, students fall prey to agents or fake promises.

Truth: If you have proper strategy, correct information and right counselling support then you can easily secure MBBS admission without donation. 

You Get MBBS Admission Without Donation?

Yes it is absolutely possible. In today’s time to get MBBS admission without donation is completely possible because of NEET-UG all systems are merit-based and transparent. 

Whether it is government or private college, admission is mostly based on NEET score and counselling process. 

Simple rule:
NEET qualify karo + counselling attend karo = No donation admission

Ways to Secure Donation-Free MBBS Admission

Government Medical Colleges

  • Most affordable option
  • Admission is based purely on NEET merit
  • Fees are very low
  • Donation is absolutely not required

High NEET scores provide direct admission without donation.

Private Colleges and Deemed Universities

  • Fees are higher, but the system is regulated.
  • Many reputable colleges offer admissions through merit and counseling.
  • All-India counseling and opportunities are available in open states.

Important: High fees do not mean donations.

These are official tuition fees, not illegal payments.

Centralized Counseling

  • MCC (Medical Counseling Committee) and state authorities conduct the process.
  • Covers 15% of All India Quota and 85% of State Quota seats.
  • The process is transparent.

By filling out the proper choice, you can get admission without any donation.

NRI / Management Quota

  • Fees are higher.
  • But seats are legally allotted through counseling.
  • Opportunities also exist during mop-up and stray vacancy rounds.

If the process is through official counseling, it is not a donation.

MBBS Abroad

  • Countries like the Philippines, Russia, and Kazakhstan offer no donations.
  • English-medium education is available.
  • Budget-friendly options are also available.

This option is useful for students with moderate NEET scores.

Entrance Exams for MBBS 2026

There is only one main entrance exam for MBBS admission in India NEET-UG.

Whether you are targeting a government college or a private one, qualifying NEET is mandatory.

NEET-UG Importance

NEET (National Eligibility cum Entrance Test) is the single gateway to MBBS admission.

  • All medical colleges in India (government and private) accept NEET scores.
  • Without NEET, MBBS admission is not possible.
  • Admission is completely merit-based (based on rank).
  • The counseling process is also based on NEET rank.

Simple understanding:

No NEET = No MBBS admission in India.

Therefore, NEET preparation is the most important step if you want to become a doctor.

Expected Timeline for MBBS 2026

The approximate timeline of NEET and admission process is something like this:

  • Application Form Release: December 2025 – January 2026
  • Admit Card: April 2026
  • NEET Exam Date: 3 May 2026
  • Result Declaration: June 2026
  • Counselling Start (MCC + State): July – August 2026
  • Admission & College Joining: August – October 2026

Eligibility Criteria for MBBS 2026

There are some basic eligibility criteria that every student must fulfill to obtain MBBS admission.

Age Limit

  • Minimum age: 17 years (as on 31 December 2026)
  • Upper age limit: Currently no strict upper limit (as per latest updates, but subject to change)

Simple: If youre 17+ then youre eligible for NEET

Subjects Required (PCB)

For MBBS you must have 12th background PCB (Physics, Chemistry, Biology)

  • Physics
  • Chemistry
  • Biology / Biotechnology
  • English (mandatory subject)

If you have not taken PCB, you are not eligible for MBBS

Minimum Marks Required

Minimum marks in 12th vary according to the category:

  • General category: 50% in PCB
  • OBC / SC / ST: 40% in PCB
  • PWD (General): 45% in PCB

Top Ways to Get MBBS Without Donation

Getting an MBBS seat without paying a donation is completely possible if you follow the right path. Here are the top options you should consider:

Government Medical Colleges

Government colleges are the best option for students looking for low-cost and donation-free MBBS admission.

  • Admission is strictly based on NEET rank and merit
  • Fees are very low compared to private institutions
  • No donation or capitation fee is involved
  • Competition is high due to limited seats

Students with a strong NEET score

Deemed Universities (Merit Quota)

Deemed universities also offer MBBS seats through merit-based counselling, especially under All India Quota.

  • Admission is done through centralized counselling (MCC)
  • No donation is required for merit quota seats
  • Tuition fees are higher than government colleges but are official and transparent
  • Wide range of college options across India

Best for: Students with moderate NEET scores who can afford higher tuition fees

MBBS Abroad (Russia, Kazakhstan, etc.)

Studying MBBS abroad is another popular option for students seeking affordable education without donation.

  • Countries like Russia, Kazakhstan, Philippines offer recognized medical programs
  • No donation or capitation fee required
  • Many universities provide English-medium courses
  • Lower competition compared to India

Students who want budget-friendly options with easier admission chances

Conclusion

MBBS admission without donation is completely possible in today’s system. With NEET-UG as the single entrance exam and centralized counselling processes, admissions have become more transparent and merit-based.

Students who focus on achieving a good NEET score, participate in proper counselling, and explore all available options such as government colleges, deemed universities, and MBBS abroad can secure a seat without paying any donation.

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

  1. Can I get MBBS admission without donation in India?
     Yes, you can get MBBS admission without donation through NEET-based merit and centralized counselling in both government and many private colleges.
  2. Is NEET compulsory for MBBS in 2026?
     Yes, NEET-UG is mandatory for MBBS admission in India as well as for studying MBBS abroad (for Indian students).
  3. Do private colleges always require donations?
     No, many private colleges offer admission through merit-based counselling. While fees may be higher, they are official and not considered donations.
  4. What is the minimum NEET score required for MBBS?
     There is no fixed score. It depends on the category, college type, and competition level each year. Higher scores increase your chances of getting a government seat.
  5. What is the difference between fees and donation?
     Fees are official charges set by the college or authority, while donation (capitation fee) is an unofficial and often illegal payment.
  6. Can I get MBBS with a low NEET score without donation?
     Yes, you can explore options like private colleges through counselling or MBBS abroad, where admission is still possible without donation.
  7.  Is MBBS abroad a good option?

Yes, if you choose a recognized university and follow guidelines, MBBS abroad can be a cost-effective and donation-free alternative.

title: Difference Between BAMS, MBBS, and BHMS: A Complete Guide for Medical Aspirants
content:
Difference Between BAMS, MBBS, and BHMS: A Complete Guide for Medical Aspirants

Many students in India dream of pursuing a career in medicine. However, when faced with options like MBBS, BAMS, and BHMS, it’s perfectly normal to feel confused. Each course has its own approach, treatment style, and career scope, so making the right decision is crucial.

First, let’s talk about MBBS. It is based on modern and evidence-based medical science. In it, you learn the diagnosis, treatment, and surgery of diseases scientifically. If you want to pursue hospital-based practice, specialization, and high-tech medical fields, MBBS is considered the best option.

BAMS (Bachelor of Ayurvedic Medicine and Surgery), on the other hand, is based on Ayurveda, a traditional Indian healing system. It focuses on herbal medicines, natural therapies, and body-mind balance. This course is perfect for students interested in natural healing and a holistic lifestyle.

Now talking about BHMS, it follows the Homeopathy system. It uses the principle of “like cures like,” where treatment is done with highly diluted medicines. This approach is gentle and is considered very effective in chronic diseases.

The duration of three courses is 5.5 years, and the NEET exam is mandatory for all. The final decision depends on your interest, career goals, and treatment philosophy. So, instead of making hasty decisions, choose your path only after understanding every option.

Overview of Each Course

This is a comprehensive 2026 comparison of the three major courses: MBBS, BAMS, and BHMS. Their treatment approaches, career options, and practical differences are clearly explained. MBBS focuses on modern medicine, BAMS focuses on Ayurveda and natural healing, while BHMS follows the gentler treatment system of homeopathy. This breakdown helps students choose the right medical career path based on their preferences and future goals.

Feature

MBBS (Allopathy)

BAMS (Ayurveda)

BHMS  (Homeopathy)

Focus

Modern Medicine & Surgery

Traditional Indian Medicine

“Like Cures Like” Principle

Duration

5.5 Years (Inc. 1yr Internship)

5.5 Years (Inc. 1yr Internship)

5.5 Years (Inc. 1yr Internship)

Entrance

NEET-UG (High Cutoff)

NEET-UG (Moderate Cutoff)

NEET-UG (Lower Cutoff)

Approach

Symptom-based Scientific Treatment

Holistic/Root Cause

Individualized Natural Remedy

Authority

Independent Surgery & Medicine

Specialized Ayur-surgery/Panchakarma

Acute/Chronic Management

Avg Starting Salary

₹6–8+ LPA (approx.)

₹3–6 LPA (approx.)

₹2.5–5 LPA (approx.)

Key Differences (Comparison Table)

Feature

BAMS

MBBS

BHMS

System of Medicine

Ayurveda

Allopathy

Homeopathy

Treatment Style

Herbal & natural

Modern medicine

Diluted remedies

Popularity

Moderate

Very High

Moderate

Career Scope

Clinics, wellness centers

Hospitals, surgery

Private practice

Difficulty Level

Moderate

High

Moderate

Eligibility Criteria 

  • NEET Qualification (Mandatory in India)

Clearing the NEET (National Eligibility cum Entrance Test) is compulsory for admission to medical courses like MBBS, BAMS, and BHMS in India. You not only need to qualify the exam but also score well, as the cut-off changes every year depending on the competition.

  • 10+2 with PCB (Physics, Chemistry, Biology)

Students must pass their 12th class with a PCB stream, preferably from a recognized board (CBSE, ICSE, or state board). Biology is the most important subject here, as it forms the foundation for further medical studies.

  • Minimum Marks Requirement

General categories typically require a minimum of 50% aggregate marks in PCB, while SC/ST/OBC categories require around 40–45%. The exact percentage may vary depending on the college and category.

Salary Comparison 

  • MBBS: MBBS graduates generally enjoy a higher starting salary, especially if they get a job in a government hospital or a reputed private hospital. After an internship, one can start at ₹40,000–₹80,000 per month, and the income increases significantly after specialization (MD/MS).
  • BAMS: BAMS doctors have a moderate salary. Starting salary can be ₹25,000–₹50,000 per month, but earnings can gradually increase with experience and setting up your own clinic, especially with the growing demand for Ayurveda.
  • BHMS: Income in BHMS depends heavily on your practice and patient base. Starting salary can be around ₹20,000–₹40,000, but earnings can increase significantly once a successful private practice is established.

Which One Should You Choose?

  • Based on Interest: First, understand your interests. If you enjoy modern science, surgeries, and hospital-based treatment, MBBS would be best. If you believe in natural healing, herbs, and a holistic lifestyle, BAMS is a strong option. However, if you prefer gentle and alternative treatment methods, you can choose BHMS.
  • Career Goals: Think about what you want to become in the future—a specialist doctor, a researcher, or running your own clinic. MBBS offers more options for specialization and hospital careers, while BAMS and BHMS offer strong scope for private practice and alternative medicine.
  • Budget and Competition Level: MBBS seats are limited, and competition is quite high. Fees are also higher in private colleges. BAMS and BHMS are comparatively affordable and there is less competition.

Conclusion

Choosing between BAMS, MBBS, and BHMS depends largely on your personal interest, career goals, and approach to healthcare. MBBS is ideal for students who want to pursue modern medicine with advanced clinical exposure and higher earning potential. On the other hand, BAMS and BHMS are excellent options for those interested in traditional and holistic healing systems like Ayurveda and Homeopathy. Check AR Group of Education for more. 

(FAQs)

1. Is NEET required for BAMS, MBBS, and BHMS?

Yes, NEET is mandatory in India for admission into all three courses BAMS, MBBS, and BHMS.

2. Which course has the highest salary?

MBBS generally offers the highest salary, especially after specialization. However, BAMS and BHMS practitioners can also earn well with experience and a successful private practice.

3. Which is better: BAMS or MBBS?

MBBS is better if you want to practice modern allopathic medicine. BAMS is a good choice if you are interested in Ayurveda and natural healing methods.

4. Can BAMS or BHMS doctors become surgeons?

No, only MBBS graduates who complete further specialization (MS/MD) can become surgeons.

5. What is the duration of these courses?

All three courses BAMS, MBBS, and BHMS have a duration of 5.5 years, including a mandatory internship.

6. Is BHMS a good career option?

Yes, BHMS can be a good career, especially if you are interested in homeopathy and willing to build your own practice.

7. Can I switch from BAMS or BHMS to MBBS?

No, you cannot directly switch. You would need to reappear for NEET and secure admission to MBBS separately.

title: Deemed University MBBS Admission Process 2026 – Complete Guide
content:
Deemed University MBBS Admission Process 2026 – Complete Guide

The MBBS admission process for deemed universities is centralized and conducted completely through NEET UG 2026. This means that students can not get admission into any deemed university without qualifying the NEET. The sole basis for admission is the NEET score, and students are allotted colleges based on their rank.

The process is managed by the Medical Counselling Committee (MCC), which fills 100% of seats in deemed universities at the all-India level. Counseling is conducted entirely online, requiring candidates to register on the official MCC website.

The main process starts through registration, where students can apply for counselling by filling their details. This is followed by the choice filling where students select their preferred deemed universities and colleges in priority order. MCC then allotted seats in multiple rounds based on NEET rank, category, and availability of seats.

After allotting the seat, then students have to report in their allotted college for document verification and fees payment. This step is very important, Otherwise your seat may also be cancelled.

Overall, deemed university’s MBBS admission is a transparent and merit-based process where NEET score and MCC counselling plays an important role. Students can secure their desired college with proper planning, correct choice filling, and timely reporting. 

NEET 2026 Eligibility Criteria

The eligibility is quite straightforward for NEET 2026. Candidates must be at least 17 years old by December 31, 2026. And after there’s no upper age limit. Candidates must have passed 12th or appear in 2026 with Physics, Chemistry, Biology/Biotechnology and English. Minimum marks in PCB are required as per category 50% for General, 40% for SC/ST/OBC, and 45% for PwD.

Key Eligibility Criteria for NEET UG 2026:

  • Minimum Age: Candidate’s age should be 17 years complete by 31 December 2026.
  • Maximum Age: There is no upper age limit, as per Supreme Court/NMC guidelines.
  • Birth Date Requirement: Candidate must be born on or before 31 December 2009.

Academic Qualification (10+2 / Qualifying Exam):

  • Mandatory Subjects: Physics, Chemistry, Biology/Biotechnology and English must be core subjects.
  • Qualifying Status: Candidate must have passed 12th or appearing for board exam in 2026.
  • NIOS/Private Candidates: NIOS or private candidates are also eligible, there was some confusion earlier but now it is allowed.

Minimum Marks/Percentile (PCB):

  • General/UR: Minimum 50% aggregate in Physics, Chemistry, Biology (PCB).
  • OBC/SC/ST: Minimum 40% aggregate in PCB.
  • PwD/General-EWS: Minimum 45% aggregate in PCB.
  • Attempts: There is no limit on the number of attempts.
  • Citizenship: Indian Nationals, NRIs, OCIs, PIOs and Foreign Nationals are all eligible for NEET 2026.

Step-by-Step Admission Process

This is the general, step-by-step, 5-stage admission process for MBBS/BDS courses in Deemed Universities through NEET UG 2026 based on previous trends and available information for the 2026 academic session.

Step 1: NEET UG 2026 Qualification

Appear & Qualify: Candidates must appear for the NEET UG 2026 exam (expected May 3, 2026) and achieve the minimum qualifying percentile.

Result: The result is usually declared in June 2026.

Step 2: MCC Registration

  • Portal: Register on the official Medical Counseling Committee (MCC) website.
  • Process: Log in with your NEET roll number, create a password, and fill in your personal details.
  • Fees: Pay a non-refundable registration fee + a refundable security deposit (approximately ₹2,00,000 for deemed universities).

Step 3: Choice Filling & Locking

  • Selection: Log in to the MCC portal and select your preferred universities and courses (MBBS/BDS) in priority order.
  • Locking: Locking your choices before the deadline is mandatory.
  • Auto-Locking: If you don’t manually lock them, the system automatically locks your last saved choices.

Step 4: Seat Allotment

  • Allotment: MCC allots seats in different rounds (Round 1, Round 2, Mop-Up, Stray Vacancy) based on NEET rank, choices and seat availability.
  • Result Format: Allotment result has been released on MCC website in PDF format.
  • Letter: After getting the seat, you have to download the provisional allotment letter.

Step 5: Reporting to College

  • Physical Reporting: You must report to the allotted college within the given time with the allotment letter and original documents.
  • Verification: Document verification takes place at the college.
  • Fee Payment: You must confirm admission by paying tuition fees.
  • Upgradation: If you are not satisfied, you can participate in the next counseling rounds to upgrade.

Counselling Rounds

Medical counselling rounds (Round 1, Round 2, Mop-up aur Stray Vacancy)  are structured phases through which MBBS/BDS seats are allotted via NEET UG 2026 and counselling is conducted by the Medical Counselling Committee. These rounds follow a step-by-step process starting from  initial allotment to filling final vacant seats. 

  1. Round 1 Counseling
  • Purpose: First seat allotment based on top merit.
  • Key Feature: A free exit option is available if a candidate does not report to the allotted college, they forfeit their seat but can participate in the next round without penalty.
  • Eligibility: All eligible candidates can participate.
  1. Round 2 Counseling
  • Purpose: To upgrade Round 1 seats or allot new candidates.
  • Key Feature: Usually, there is no free exit. If a seat is allotted, joining is mandatory. If you accept a new seat, your Round 1 seat is automatically cancelled.
  • Eligibility: Candidates who did not receive a seat in Round 1, resigned, or applied for an upgrade.
  1. Mop-Up Round
  • Purpose: To fill the remaining vacant seats after Round 1 and Round 2.
  • Key Feature: Mostly conducted for deemed universities, central universities, and some state quota seats. In some cases, candidates who have already joined Round 2 cannot participate.
  • Eligibility: Generally, candidates who did not secure a seat in any of the previous rounds.
  1. Stray Vacancy Round
  • Purpose: To fill the remaining vacant seats after Mop-Up.
  • Key Feature: This round is usually conducted at the college/institution level (either online or offline), based on the list prepared by the counseling authority.
  • Risk: This round is highly competitive. If a seat is allotted, it is mandatory to take it, otherwise strict penalties (such as debarring) may apply.

Documents Required

  • NEET Scorecard
  • Admit Card
  • 10th & 12th Marksheet
  • ID Proof (Aadhar/PAN)
  • Passport size photos
  • Allotment letter 

MBBS Fees in Deemed Universities 

MBBS fees at deemed medical colleges for the 2026–27 academic session are quite high. Annual tuition fees for management quota seats generally range from ₹15 lakh to ₹30 lakh+ per year. The admission process is conducted through NEET UG 2026, and counseling is handled by the Medical Counseling Committee.

Fee Structure Overview:

  • Total Course Package:

The full 4.5-year MBBS tuition cost can range from approximately ₹70 lakh to ₹1.6 crore.

  • High-End Fees:

Some premium colleges charge ₹25–30 lakh per year (e.g., DY Patil Medical College, Amrita Institute of Medical Sciences).

  • Mid-Range Fees:

Many colleges range from ₹15–18 lakh per year (e.g., KMC Manipal, JSS Medical College).

  • NRI Quota Seats:

Fees for NRI/foreign quota seats are even higher approx. USD 40,000 to USD 85,000+ per year.

  • Annual Hikes:

Many deemed universities increase fees by 5%–10% every year.

  • Additional Costs:

Hostel, mess, development fees, and security deposit are separately charged, which add several lakhs to the total cost.

Mode of Payment:

  • Annual/Semester-wise Payment:

Fees are usually paid yearly or semester-wise.

  • Demand Draft (DD):

Traditionally the most common method—a DD is made out in the name of the university/college (generally from a nationalized bank).

  • RTGS/NEFT/IMPS:

Direct bank transfer is a safe and fast option for large amounts.

  • Online Payment Portal:

Many universities offer online payment options on their websites—Debit Card, Credit Card, Net Banking, UPI, etc.

NEET Counselling Key Points

For 100% All India Quota (AIQ) counseling especially for deemed and central universities choice filling is the most important factor. Fees are usually high, and refund rules become quite strict after Round 2.

Key Takeaways for NEET UG 2026 Counselling:

100% All India Quota (AIQ): Counseling is conducted by the Medical Counseling Committee (MCC). It covers 15% government seats + 100% seats of deemed universities, central universities, AIIMS and JIPMER.

Fees Structure: The fees of deemed universities are significantly higher than those of government/state colleges. Therefore, it is important to clearly understand the total cost (tuition + hostel + other charges) before seeking admission.

Choice Filling Strategy: Only those colleges that you include in your choice list will be considered. Therefore, fill your preferences carefully keeping in mind college reputation, location, and fees. MCC generally conducts 4 rounds (Round 1, Round 2, Mop-up/3rd Round, and Stray Vacancy).

Refund & Exit Rules:

  • Round 1: Free exit is available there is no penalty for leaving the seat.
  • Round 2 & After: If you join a seat, you cannot leave it afterward.
  • If you do not join after the seat is allotted, your security deposit is forfeited.
  • Registration: Always register for counseling on the official Medical Counseling Committee (MCC) website and keep checking for updates.

Conclusion

The Deemed University MBBS Admission Process 2026 is a completely centralized process conducted on the basis of NEET UG rank. Candidates have to follow the steps of registration, choice filling, and seat allotment through MCC counseling. Since the fees of deemed universities are comparatively high, students should fill their preferences carefully and attend counseling with proper planning. With the right strategy and timely decisions, students can secure admission to their desired medical college.

FAQs 

  1. Is NEET mandatory for MBBS admission to deemed universities?

Yes, qualifying NEET UG is compulsory. Admission without a NEET score is not possible.

  1. Who conducts counseling for deemed universities?

Counseling is conducted centrally through the Medical Counseling Committee (MCC).

  1. Is there a state quota for deemed universities?

No, 100% of seats in deemed universities are filled on an all-India basis; there is no state quota.

  1. How many counseling rounds are there?

Generally, there are four rounds: Round 1, Round 2, Mop-up Round, and Stray Vacancy Round.

  1. Are fees refundable?

There is a partial refund policy, but rules vary depending on the round and timing.

  1. Is choice filling important?

Absolutely! The correct choice filling strategy increases your chances of getting a better college.

  1. What are the fees for deemed universities?

Fees are quite high, generally ranging between ₹10 lakh and ₹25 lakh per year (depending on the college).
